exercises.sourceTaskName: Language Work, Task 8: The prefixes e- and cyber-

promptParts.after: is an employee who uses his company's internet connection during work hours to chat with friends, play games, etc.

questions.grammarRule: 'Cyberslacker' (cyber- + slacker) describes someone who wastes time online instead of working.

promptParts.after: is a postcard sent via the Internet.

questions.grammarRule: The e- prefix means 'electronic'; an 'e-card' is an electronic version of a postcard/greeting card.

promptParts.after: is a small magazine or newsletter published online.

questions.grammarRule: 'E-zine' (electronic + magazine) is a magazine or newsletter published online.

promptParts.after: you can use computers with internet access for a fee.

questions.grammarRule: The cyber- prefix relates to computer networks; a 'cybercafe' is a cafe where you pay to use internet-connected computers.

promptParts.after: include internet fraud, digital piracy, theft of confidential information etc.

questions.grammarRule: 'Cybercrime' describes criminal activities carried out using computers/networks, like fraud and piracy.

promptParts.before: In the future, all elections will be carried out using

questions.grammarRule: 'E-voting' (electronic voting) refers to casting votes using electronic systems instead of paper ballots.

promptParts.before: You can now sign legal documents online using an

questions.grammarRule: An 'e-signature' (electronic signature) is used to sign documents digitally, replacing a handwritten signature.

promptParts.after: will revolutionize the way we take exams.

questions.grammarRule: 'E-assessment' (electronic assessment) refers to conducting tests/exams using computers instead of paper.

promptParts.after: can be used on some websites instead of real money to make purchases. It reduces the risk of fraud.

questions.grammarRule: 'E-cash' (electronic cash) is digital money used for online purchases instead of physical currency.

promptParts.after: is like the paper version, but in digital form.

questions.grammarRule: An 'e-book' (electronic book) is the digital version of a printed book.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Grammar and Vocabulary Exercises, Task 1: don't have to or mustn't

questions.grammarRule: 'Mustn't' expresses prohibition (rude words are forbidden), not 'don't have to' (which means something is merely optional).

promptParts.after: finish the exercise now. You can finish it at home.

questions.grammarRule: 'Don't have to' means something is not necessary/optional (you can finish it later); 'mustn't' would wrongly imply it's forbidden.

promptParts.after: go to church if he doesn't want to.

questions.grammarRule: 'Doesn't have to' (third person singular) means something is optional, matching 'if he doesn't want to'; 'mustn't' would wrongly mean he's forbidden to go.

promptParts.after: drive so aggressively. You'll end up killing someone.

questions.grammarRule: 'Mustn't' expresses prohibition for dangerous behavior; 'don't have to' would wrongly suggest it's just optional rather than something to avoid.

promptParts.after: answer your emails. There's no law about it.

questions.grammarRule: 'Doesn't have to' means something isn't obligatory, matching 'There's no law about it'; 'mustn't' would wrongly mean he's forbidden.

promptParts.after: pay now. You can pay me at the end of the month.

questions.grammarRule: 'Don't have to' means something isn't necessary right now (you can pay later); 'mustn't' would wrongly mean you're forbidden to pay now.

promptParts.after: stay out so late without ringing me. I was worried sick.

questions.grammarRule: 'Mustn't' here expresses a strong scolding/prohibition ('you shouldn't do that'), not mere optionality.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 2: Prepositions of Time (at, in, on)

questions.grammarRule: 'On' + day of the week (plural for a habitual pattern): 'on Saturdays'.
'In' + month: 'in March'.

promptParts.before: Valencia is too hot for me

questions.grammarRule: 'In' + season: 'in summer'.

promptParts.after: Sunday mornings I have coffee and toast for breakfast and read the newspaper.

questions.grammarRule: 'On' + a specific part of a specific day: 'on Sunday mornings'.

promptParts.after: Monday and Wednesday evenings I go to English class.

questions.grammarRule: 'On' + a specific part of specific days: 'on Monday and Wednesday evenings'.
'At' + clock time: 'at 2:30 pm'.
'In' + year: 'in 1492'.

promptParts.after: night but I prefer the morning.

questions.grammarRule: 'At night' is a fixed exception (unlike 'in the morning/afternoon/evening').

promptParts.before: I love watching the James Bond film

questions.grammarRule: 'On' + a specific named day: 'on Christmas Day' (contrast with 'at Christmas' for the holiday period generally).
'At' + clock time: 'at 10 o'clock'.
'In the week' is a fixed phrase meaning 'during weekdays'.

promptParts.before: We usually go out for dinner

questions.grammarRule: 'At the weekend' is the standard British English phrase (American English typically uses 'on the weekend').

promptParts.before: She's always very grumpy first thing

questions.grammarRule: 'In the morning/afternoon/evening' is the standard pattern (contrast with the exception 'at night').

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 3: Prepositions of Place (at, in, on, to)

questions.grammarRule: 'Get to' + place describes arriving/reaching somewhere (motion): 'get to work'.
'Arrive at' + a building/small place: 'arrive at work'. ('Arrive in' is used for larger areas like cities/countries.)

promptParts.after: Paris at five in the morning.

questions.grammarRule: 'Arrive in' + a city/large area: 'arrived in Paris'.
'In bed' is the standard fixed phrase.

promptParts.before: Clare's boyfriend's a nurse. He works

questions.grammarRule: 'Work at' + a workplace: 'works at the hospital'.

promptParts.before: A: When's Jenny having her baby? B: Very soon. She's already

questions.grammarRule: 'In hospital' (without 'the', British English) means being admitted as a patient.
'On' + surface: 'on the table'.

promptParts.before: I'm sorry. María's not here. She's

questions.grammarRule: 'At work' describes being present at your workplace.
'Go to' + place describes movement/destination: 'go to school'.
'At' + institution/building where an action takes place: 'have lunch at school'.
'Be at' + a workplace: 'be at the office'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 4: as long, providing and unless

questions.grammarRule: 'Providing that' (= 'on condition that') introduces a positive condition: she's sweet on the condition you don't contradict her.

promptParts.after: you're prepared to buy me a new one if you break it.

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' (= 'if not') introduces a negative condition: you can't borrow it if you're NOT prepared to replace it.

promptParts.after: that you buy me a new one if you break it.

questions.grammarRule: 'Providing that' introduces a positive condition: you can borrow it on the condition you'd replace it.

promptParts.after: as you don't forget the torch and the can-opener.

questions.grammarRule: 'As long as' introduces a positive condition, similar to 'providing': camping is fun on the condition you remember those items.

promptParts.after: you forget the torch and the can-opener.

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' introduces a negative condition: camping is fun if you DON'T forget those items.
'Unless' introduces a negative condition: don't use them if they're NOT free.
'As long as' introduces a positive condition: only use them on the condition they're free.
'Unless' introduces a negative condition: I refuse if you DON'T come with me (i.e. I'll only go if you come).

promptParts.before: A great white shark will be nice to you

questions.grammarRule: 'As long as' introduces a positive (if silly) condition: the shark is nice on the condition you stroke its nose.

promptParts.after: that the weather is good, we'll spend the day in the mountains.

questions.grammarRule: 'Providing that' introduces a positive condition at the start of a sentence: on the condition the weather is good.

promptParts.before: I refuse to watch Channel Two,

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' introduces a negative condition: I refuse if they DON'T show fewer adverts.

promptParts.after: I can find a cheaper one, I'll have to buy that monitor.

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' introduces a negative condition at the start of a sentence: if I CAN'T find a cheaper one, I'll have to buy it.

promptParts.after: as you wear a bullet-proof jacket, being a president is a safe job.

questions.grammarRule: 'As long as' introduces a positive condition: the job is safe on the condition you wear protection.

promptParts.after: you're prepared to work very hard, you'll never be a doctor.

questions.grammarRule: 'Unless' introduces a negative condition: you'll never be a doctor if you're NOT prepared to work hard.
